sections.content: Overview

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in its advanced forms tackles a critical problem that pure language models cannot solve independently: producing factually grounded, domain-specific, and temporally current responses. Standard large language models are frozen at training time, contain no persistent knowledge of private or proprietary information, and often hallucinate plausible-sounding but false information when queried on specialized domains. RAG Part 4 focuses on the sophisticated techniques required to scale RAG systems in production: dynamic document indexing strategies that handle continuously updating knowledge bases, advanced ranking and reranking mechanisms that balance relevance with diversity and freshness, integration of feedback loops that train retrieval components from user interactions, and optimization techniques that reduce latency while maintaining quality. Without these advanced techniques, naive RAG implementations suffer from catastrophic retrieval failures where the wrong documents are ranked first, outdated information poisons the generation pipeline, and system latency explodes under load. This lesson covers the architectural and algorithmic innovations that separate production-grade RAG systems from prototype implementations.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Virat Kohli preparing for a crucial Test match against Australia. Before the match, he doesn't just rely on his inherent batting muscle memory—he needs his coaching team to continuously fetch updated video analysis of Pat Cummings' latest bowling patterns, field placements from the last two series, and real-time weather reports from Melbourne Cricket Ground. During the innings, his batting strategy isn't static; it dynamically adjusts based on which bowlers have been performing best in THIS specific match (not just in their career statistics), and his coaches provide live feedback—if the strategy against Cummings isn't working after three overs, they immediately rerank their priorities and try aggressive batting to disrupt his rhythm. The retrieval system (the coaching team's research) must work faster than the generation system (Kohli's batting decisions) or the entire strategy collapses; they need mechanisms to rank information by recency (this series's data beats last year's data), filter out misleading patterns from small sample sizes (maiden overs don't predict the economy for a full spell), and incorporate feedback from live match performance to improve retrieval for the next innings. Without continuous updating and intelligent reranking, Kohli would be batting on outdated opposition analysis and stale field intelligence—leading to poor shot selection and dismissal. This reveals why RAG Part 4 isn't just about retrieving documents, but about dynamically managing, ranking, and learning from information retrieval in real time.
Core Concepts
Dynamic Document Indexing and Incremental Updates

Dynamic document indexing addresses the practical reality that knowledge bases in production are never static. Traditional information retrieval systems built indexes at training time; RAG systems must support continuous ingestion of new documents, updates to existing documents, and deletion of outdated or irrelevant material while maintaining search performance. Incremental indexing strategies fall into two categories: append-only systems that add new documents to existing indexes without rebuilding (using techniques like Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs for vector similarity search), and update-aware systems that track document versions and relevance timestamps. The challenge intensifies when dealing with dense vector embeddings: each new document requires encoding with a high-dimensional embedding model, then insertion into a vector database with fast approximate nearest neighbor search. Systems must handle scenarios where a document's importance changes over time (a news article becomes more relevant on its anniversary), where documents supersede previous versions (a drug approval announcement invalidates prior analysis), and where bulk deletions occur (privacy regulations like GDPR require purging personal data). Failure modes include stale document retrieval where old information is ranked higher than newer corrections, index corruption from concurrent update conflicts, and catastrophic performance degradation if indexes aren't properly optimized for update-heavy workloads. Production systems like Pinecone and Weaviate provide serverless vector database capabilities precisely to abstract these complexity layers away from application developers.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the ICC maintaining the official world cricket statistics database that feeds into every broadcast commentary system. At the start of each day's play, millions of cricket fans worldwide depend on live scoreboards that must update the database every single delivery—Rohit Sharma scores a boundary, the run count increments; he's dismissed for 89, the batting scorecard is finalized and his dismissal analysis added; a new batsman walks in and their career statistics must be instantly searchable. The index isn't rebuilt from scratch each morning; instead, incremental updates append new match records to existing indexes. However, when a controversial DRS decision overturns an earlier wicket decision (like a ball given out that's actually not out), the system must retroactively correct that player's statistics, invalidating cached analysis that depended on the original decision. When a player retires, their active-player index entries must be marked as retired-player entries—similar to how a document transitions from 'active medical device' to 'discontinued product' in a pharmaceutical knowledge base. If the indexing system isn't sophisticated enough, commentators might still read from outdated statistics ("Bumrah has taken 50 Test wickets this year" when he's actually taken 75 after the latest match), leading to broadcast errors. The system must handle millions of concurrent read requests from scoreboard displays while simultaneously processing thousands of write operations from live match feeds without index corruption. This reveals that RAG systems, like live sports statistics systems, must maintain both speed and accuracy under continuous update pressure—indexing strategies aren't a one-time setup but an ongoing operational concern.
Multi-Stage Ranking and Reranking

Multi-stage ranking pipelines address a fundamental asymmetry in RAG: the retriever must be fast (sub-millisecond) to scale to millions of documents, but often uses approximate similarity metrics that sacrifice accuracy; the reranker can afford higher latency because it operates on a small candidate set, but must be precise. A typical pipeline retrieves the top-k documents using a lightweight embedding model (e.g., sentence-transformers), then applies one or more reranking stages: a dense reranker fine-tuned on relevance pairs, a sparse reranker using BM25 for keyword overlap, a learning-to-rank model that combines multiple signals (freshness, popularity, semantic similarity), and domain-specific heuristics that boost certain documents. The Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) technique elegantly combines rankings from multiple retrieval strategies: if embedding-based retrieval ranks a document at position 3 and keyword-based retrieval ranks it at position 5, RRF formula weights the combined score such that both signals contribute without one drowning out the other. Diversity reranking prevents the pathological case where the top-10 results are all variants of the same document; maximal marginal relevance (MMR) reranking penalizes documents that are too similar to already-selected results. Cross-encoder rerankers (like BERT-based sentence-pair classifiers) compute a relevance score for each query-document pair, outperforming dual-encoder approaches in quality but at higher computational cost. The critical insight is that retrieval and reranking serve different purposes: retrieval achieves recall (finding all possibly relevant documents), while reranking achieves precision (surfacing the best documents for this specific query). A retriever with 50% recall but a perfect reranker still fails; a retriever with 100% recall but a broken reranker wastes computation.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: During the IPL mega-auction, selectors employ a multi-stage bidding strategy to identify the best players within budget constraints. First, they run a fast approximate filter using publicly available statistics—filtering all-rounders who've batted in the top-order in at least 10 T20 matches this season and have a strike rate above 140 (this is the retriever stage, emphasizing speed and recall). This fast filter might return 40 candidates. Next, a committee of expert coaches watches video of the top 15 candidates from this list, assessing their recent form, technique against specific bowlers, fitness condition, and team chemistry fit (this is the reranker stage, using expensive human judgment on a smaller set). Then, the chief selector applies a diversity constraint: they don't want three similar left-arm spinners; they reorder candidates so that if they've already selected Yuzvendra Chahal, a similar spinner like Kuldeep Yadav gets deprioritized even if his individual stats are marginally better (this is MMR reranking, preventing redundancy). Finally, they cross-check against the team's balanced composition needs—if the team desperately needs openers, they rescore candidates upward if they're reliable opening batsmen, even if they're slightly worse all-rounders overall (this is learning-to-rank, combining multiple signals). Without this multi-stage approach, they'd either miss great players buried in the initial 100 candidates (weak recall) or waste auction money bidding on 40 candidates without proper vetting (wasteful precision). This reveals why RAG systems use retrieval for breadth and reranking for depth—you can't judge quality accurately at scale, so you filter at scale first, then judge carefully on the filtered set.
Feedback Loops and Implicit Learning from User Interactions

Feedback loops in RAG systems transform one-shot retrieval into a learning system that improves over time through observational data. The core mechanism: when a user accepts a generated answer, it signals that the retrieved documents were relevant; when a user rejects an answer or modifies it, it signals retrieval failure or hallucination. This implicit feedback (click-through rates, dwell time, explicit relevance ratings, copy-paste behavior) trains reward models that predict whether a retrieved document will be useful for a given query. In production, implicit feedback vastly outweighs explicit ratings because users rarely annotate—but extracting signal from noisy implicit data is technically challenging. Contextual bandits algorithms balance exploration (retrieving diverse documents that might teach the system new patterns) and exploitation (retrieving documents the system is already confident about). Thompson sampling over document embeddings enables the system to maintain uncertainty estimates: if three documents seem equally relevant by embedding similarity, Thompson sampling ensures all three are retrieved across different user sessions, and whichever one receives positive feedback gets higher weight in future rankings. Hard negative mining identifies documents that are retrieved despite being irrelevant—a query about 'apple fruit' should not retrieve 'Apple Inc.' as the top result, yet embedding-based systems often fail here. Mining these hard negatives from user interactions and fine-tuning embeddings on them dramatically improves precision. Temporal decay functions weight recent feedback more heavily (feedback from last week matters more than feedback from 6 months ago, as document quality and relevance evolve). The challenge intensifies in multi-turn conversations where earlier user queries influence later context; feedback on turn 5 might actually apply to retrieval decisions in turn 1.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket team's fielding captain learns from every ball bowled in a series, progressively improving field placement and bowling changes. In the first innings against England, Rishabh Pant (captain) places fielders based on pre-match analysis (the initial retriever—fast, approximate). When Joe Root hits three consecutive boundaries through the covers against pace bowling, this is negative feedback—the retrieved strategy (fast pace bowling with mid-off inside) isn't working against Root's technique. By the second innings, Pant retrieves updated information from his scouts: "Root struggled against short-pitched bowling earlier in the tour; here's video evidence of him being uncomfortable to the short ball." He reranks his bowling strategy, promoting Bumrah's short-pitched spells higher in priority. But he also maintains exploration: he still tries one spell of fuller length bowling (exploration) because Root might adapt, and one successful fuller delivery provides information that helps in future series. When Root plays a poor shot to a fuller ball and gets dismissed, this explicit feedback updates Pant's mental model—the DRS evidence of the dismissal directly trains his future strategy. Over five Tests, Pant accumulates feedback from Root's performance against different lengths, speeds, and field placements. By the final Test, his field placements and bowling selections have implicitly learned from every interaction: Root faces shorter deliveries more often, finer fielders appear when the danger zone is the cover region, and bowlers who've succeeded against Root historically get more chances. However, Pant knows feedback has a temporal decay—how Root performed in Test 1 is less informative than how he performed in Test 4 when possibly fatigued. If Pant over-weights feedback from Test 1, he might set a field for fresh Root rather than tired Root, leading to poor strategy in Test 5. This reveals that RAG systems, like cricket strategy, must learn from interaction data while managing exploration-exploitation tradeoffs and temporal relevance of that feedback.
Latency Optimization and Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search

Latency optimization in RAG systems addresses a hard constraint: generating a high-quality answer requires sub-second end-to-end latency, but searching a 10-million-document corpus for truly nearest neighbors is computationally prohibitive. Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms sacrifice 1-2% accuracy in exchange for 100-1000x speedup compared to exact nearest neighbor search. The Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) algorithm constructs a multi-layer graph where each node is a document embedding; starting from a random layer, the search greedily moves toward the query embedding, exponentially reducing search scope with each layer descent. HNSW achieves O(log N) search complexity for N documents with tunable parameters controlling the memory-accuracy tradeoff. Product Quantization (PQ) compresses embeddings: rather than storing full 768-dimensional vectors, documents are stored as quantized codes (64-128 bytes) that can be rapidly compared. Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH) maps high-dimensional embeddings to low-dimensional binary codes preserving similarity: documents with similar embeddings hash to the same buckets with high probability. Hybrid search systems combine vector and keyword matching: a document matching the query keywords gets a multiplicative boost to its embedding score, capturing semantic relevance while maintaining keyword precision. The tradeoff surface is critical: HNSW excels with static indexes but struggles with frequent updates; product quantization reduces memory by 10x but introduces quantization error that compounds through reranking stages; LSH hashing loses distance information (only approximate). Failure modes include the cache-line thrashing problem where random memory access patterns in large-scale ANN search exceed CPU cache hit rates, causing unpredictable latency spikes. Production systems deploy ANN algorithms aggressively: a 50ms retrieval stage on a 100ms latency budget is unacceptable, pushing systems toward GPU-accelerated ANN search (FAISS library) or specialized hardware (TPUs for embedding computation).

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket analyst must instantly answer live commentary questions during a T20 match: "How many times has Jasprit Bumrah been hit for six by left-handed batsmen in powerplay overs in T20 cricket?" Exact search means reviewing every single ball Bumrah has bowled to every left-handed batsman in powerplay—potentially 10,000+ videos to analyze exactly. This takes hours, which is useless during a live broadcast. Instead, the analyst uses approximate search: first, filter to left-handed batsmen (keyword filter, like LSH binning)—eliminates 60% of irrelevant videos instantly. Second, find the 50 most similar matches by tournament, venue, season, and Bumrah's recent form (HNSW-like layered approach: quickly eliminate obviously dissimilar seasons, then fine-grain search within plausible seasons). Third, within those 50 matches, count six-hitting instances (exact search on a small candidate set). This approximate approach sacrifices a tiny amount of accuracy (might miss one relevant video from an obscure 2017 match) but returns answers in 5 seconds instead of 3 hours. The analyst also caches frequently asked questions: Bumrah's powerplay economy against left-handers is precomputed and stored as a summary (product quantization—compressed statistics instead of raw video data), so repeat questions return instantly from cache. During the match, as Bumrah bowls new balls, the analyst adds new vectors to the index dynamically; they use an insert-optimized structure where new data points are added to the fast layer of the hierarchy first, then percolated down later (HNSW update strategy), preventing index rebuilds that would cause broadcast lag. This reveals why RAG systems use approximate algorithms: perfect retrieval from massive corpora is computationally impossible, so RAG trades tiny accuracy losses for enormous latency gains that are essential for interactive systems.

import numpy as np
from typing import List, Dict, Tuple
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import heapq

# Cricket-themed RAG system demonstrating advanced concepts

@dataclass
class CricketMatch:
    """Represents a cricket match document in the knowledge base"""
    match_id: str
    title: str
    content: str
    embedding: np.ndarray  # Dense vector representation
    timestamp: datetime
    match_type: str  # Test, ODI, T20
    teams: Tuple[str, str]
    relevance_score: float = 0.0

@dataclass
class RetrievalResult:
    """Ranking result from multi-stage retrieval pipeline"""
    match_id: str
    title: str
    embedding_rank: int
    keyword_rank: int
    reranked_score: float
    freshness_boost: float
    final_rank: int

class DynamicCricketRAGSystem:
    """Advanced RAG system with dynamic indexing, multi-stage ranking, and feedback loops"""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.embedding_dim:
int = 768):
        self.embedding_dim = embedding_dim
        self.documents: Dict[str, CricketMatch] = {}
        self.feedback_log: List[Dict] = []  # Implicit feedback from users
        self.learned_hard_negatives: List[Tuple[str, str]] = []  # Query, bad_doc pairs
        self.exploration_rate = 0.15  # Thompson sampling exploration
        
    def add_or_update_document(self
sections.match:
CricketMatch):
        """
        Dynamically add or update a cricket match document in the index.
        Demonstrates incremental indexing without full rebuild.
        """
        self.documents[match.match_id] = match
        print(f"[DYNAMIC INDEX] Added/updated match {match.match_id}: {match.title}")
        # In production, this would update vector DB indexes (Pinecone, Weaviate)
        # HNSW layers would be rebalanced incrementally rather than rebuilt
    
    def retrieve_initial_candidates(self
sections.top_k:
int = 10) -> List[Tuple[str, float, float]]:
        """
        Stage 1: Fast retrieval using approximate nearest neighbor search.
        Returns (match_id, embedding_similarity, keyword_score) for top_k candidates.
        """
        embedding_scores = []
        for match_id, doc in self.documents.items():
            # Simulate HNSW-like ANN: approximate cosine similarity
            sim = np.dot(query_embedding, doc.embedding) / (
                np.linalg.norm(query_embedding) * np.linalg.norm(doc.embedding) + 1e-8
            )
            embedding_scores.append((match_id, sim))
        
        # Keyword filtering (LSH-like binning)
        keyword_scores = []
        for match_id, doc in self.documents.items():
            # Simple keyword overlap as proxy for LSH
            query_terms = set(keyword_query.lower().split())
            doc_terms = set((doc.title + " " + doc.content).lower().split())
            overlap = len(query_terms & doc_terms) / (len(query_terms) + 1e-8)
            keyword_scores.append((match_id, overlap))
        
        # Separate rankings
        embedding_scores.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        keyword_scores.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        
        embedding_ranked = {match_id: i for i, (match_id, _) in enumerate(embedding_scores)}
        keyword_ranked = {match_id: i for i, (match_id, _) in enumerate(keyword_scores)}
        
        # Return top-k candidates from union of both rankings
        candidates = []
        for match_id in list(embedding_ranked.keys())[:top_k * 2]:  # Retrieve extra for reranking
            emb_sim = dict(embedding_scores)[match_id]
            kw_score = dict(keyword_scores)[match_id]
            candidates.append((match_id, emb_sim, kw_score))
        
        return candidates[:top_k]
    
    def rerank_with_diversity(self
sections.candidates:
List[Tuple[str, float, float]],
                             top_k: int = 5) -> List[RetrievalResult]:
        """
        Stage 2: Rerank with diversity constraint (Maximal Marginal Relevance).
        Prevents redundant similar documents from dominating top results.
        """
        selected = []
        remaining = list(candidates)
        
        for rank in range(min(top_k, len(candidates))):
            if not remaining:
                break
            
            best_idx = 0
            best_mmr_score = -np.inf
            
            for i, (match_id, emb_sim, kw_sim) in enumerate(remaining):
                # MMR = relevance - λ * max_similarity_to_already_selected
                relevance = 0.7 * emb_sim + 0.3 * kw_sim
                
                if not selected:
                    diversity_penalty = 0
                else:
                    # Calculate similarity to already selected docs
                    max_sim_to_selected = max([
                        np.dot(self.documents[match_id].embedding,
                              self.documents[selected_match_id].embedding) / (
                          np.linalg.norm(self.documents[match_id].embedding) *
                          np.linalg.norm(self.documents[selected_match_id].embedding) + 1e-8
                        )
                        for selected_match_id, _, _, _, _, _ in selected
                    ])
                    diversity_penalty = 0.5 * max_sim_to_selected  # λ = 0.5
                
                mmr_score = relevance - diversity_penalty
                if mmr_score > best_mmr_score:
                    best_mmr_score = mmr_score
                    best_idx = i
            
            match_id, emb_sim, kw_sim = remaining.pop(best_idx)
            selected.append((match_id, len(selected), emb_sim, kw_sim, best_mmr_score, 0.0))
        
        return selected
    
    def apply_freshness_boost(self

List[Tuple[str, int, float, float, float, float]],
                             recency_weight: float = 0.1) -> List[RetrievalResult]:
        """
        Stage 3: Apply temporal decay and freshness boosting.
        Newer documents get boosted unless explicitly marked as superseded.
        """
        now = datetime.now()
        results = []
        
        for match_id, emb_rank, emb_sim, kw_sim, mmr_score, _ in candidates:
            doc = self.documents[match_id]
            
            # Temporal decay: documents older than 30 days get linearly reduced weight
            age_days = (now - doc.timestamp).days
            freshness_factor = max(0.5, 1.0 - (age_days / 30.0) * recency_weight)
            freshness_boost = freshness_factor - 1.0  # Boost is delta from 1.0
            
            # Apply RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) combining embedding and keyword rankings
            rrf_score = (1.0 / (60 + emb_rank)) + (1.0 / (60 + int(emb_rank)))  # Simulate keyword rank
            final_score = rrf_score * freshness_factor
            
            # Check if this is a hard negative from feedback (learning signal)
            is_hard_negative = any(match_id == neg[1] for neg in self.learned_hard_negatives)
            if is_hard_negative:
                final_score *= 0.3  # Penalize hard negatives learned from feedback
            
            results.append(RetrievalResult(
                match_id=match_id,
                title=doc.title,
                embedding_rank=emb_rank,
                keyword_rank=emb_rank,  # Simplified
                reranked_score=final_score,
                freshness_boost=freshness_boost,
                final_rank=0
            ))
        
        # Sort by final score
        results.sort(key=lambda x: x.reranked_score, reverse=True)
        for i, result in enumerate(results):
            result.final_rank = i + 1
        
        return results
    
    def record_feedback(self
sections.user_accepted:
bool,
                       dwell_time_seconds: float):
        """
        Record implicit feedback from user interactions.
        Used to train hard negatives and improve future retrieval.
        """
        feedback = {
            'timestamp': datetime.now()

sections.dwell_time:
dwell_time_seconds
        }
        self.feedback_log.append(feedback)
        
        # Learn hard negatives: if user rejected quickly, mark as hard negative
        if not user_accepted and dwell_time_seconds < 3:
            self.learned_hard_negatives.append((query, retrieved_match_id))
            print(f"[LEARNING] Hard negative learned: query='{query}' != doc={retrieved_match_id}")
    
    def retrieve_with_exploration(self
sections.top_k:
int = 5) -> List[RetrievalResult]:
        """
        Thompson sampling: balance exploitation (retrieve what works) with exploration
        (retrieve uncertain candidates to learn more).
        """
        # Stage 1: Initial retrieval
        candidates = self.retrieve_initial_candidates(query_embedding, keyword_query, top_k * 3)
        
        # Stage 2: Diversity reranking
        diverse_candidates = self.rerank_with_diversity(candidates, top_k * 2)
        
        # Stage 3: Freshness and feedback integration
        final_results = self.apply_freshness_boost(diverse_candidates, recency_weight=0.1)
        
        # Thompson sampling: occasionally replace top result with uncertain candidate
        if np.random.random() < self.exploration_rate and len(final_results) > 1:
            # Swap top result with random other result for exploration
            exploration_idx = np.random.randint(1, len(final_results))
            final_results[0], final_results[exploration_idx] = final_results[exploration_idx], final_results[0]
            print(f"[EXPLORATION] Thompson sampling triggered, exploring alternative result")
        
        return final_results[:top_k]


# Example usage demonstrating the full RAG pipeline
def main():
    rag = DynamicCricketRAGSystem(embedding_dim=768)
    
    # Create sample cricket match documents with synthetic embeddings
    matches = [
        CricketMatch(
            match_id="ind_aus_test_2023_01",
            title="India vs Australia Test 2023 - Bumrah Bowling Analysis",
            content="Jasprit Bumrah's exceptional bowling performance with 7 wickets for 45 runs",
            embedding=np.random.randn(768),
            timestamp=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=5),
            match_type="Test",
            teams=("India", "Australia")
        ),
        CricketMatch(
            match_id="ipl_2024_rr_vs_csk",
            title="IPL 2024 - Rajasthan Royals vs CSK Powerplay Tactics",
            content="Aggressive powerplay strategy with emphasis on pace bowling variations",
            embedding=np.random.randn(768),
            timestamp=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=2),
            match_type="T20",
            teams=("Rajasthan Royals", "Chennai Super Kings")
        ),
        CricketMatch(
            match_id="wc_2023_final_analysis",
            title="World Cup 2023 Final - Kohli's Match-Winning Innings",
            content="Detailed analysis of Virat Kohli's 120-run innings that secured victory",
            embedding=np.random.randn(768),
            timestamp=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=45),
            match_type="ODI",
            teams=("India", "Pakistan")
        ),
        CricketMatch(
            match_id="psl_2024_pace_bowling",
            title="PSL 2024 - Death Bowling and Economy Analysis",
            content="Death bowling strategies across six teams in PSL season",
            embedding=np.random.randn(768),
            timestamp=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=1),
            match_type="T20",
            teams=("Multiple", "Multiple")
        )
    ]
    
    # Dynamic indexing: add documents
    for match in matches:
        rag.add_or_update_document(match)
    
    # Simulate a query about Bumrah's recent performance
    query_embedding = np.random.randn(768)
    keyword_query = "Bumrah bowling performance recent"
    
    print("\n[RETRIEVAL PIPELINE] Starting multi-stage retrieval...")
    results = rag.retrieve_with_exploration(query_embedding, keyword_query, top_k=3)
    
    print("\n[RESULTS] Top 3 reranked matches:")
    for result in results:
        print(f"  Rank {result.final_rank}: {result.title}")
        print(f"    - Reranked Score: {result.reranked_score:.4f}")
        print(f"    - Freshness Boost: {result.freshness_boost:.4f}")
    
    # Simulate user feedback (implicit learning)
    print("\n[FEEDBACK] Recording user interactions...")
    rag.record_feedback(keyword_query, results[0].match_id, user_accepted=True, dwell_time_seconds=12.5)
    rag.record_feedback(keyword_query, "wc_2023_final_analysis", user_accepted=False, dwell_time_seconds=1.2)
    
    print(f"\nFeedback logged: {len(rag.feedback_log)} interactions")
    print(f"Hard negatives learned: {len(rag.learned_hard_negatives)} pairs")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

sections.content: The code demonstrates all four advanced RAG concepts working together. The `DynamicCricketRAGSystem` class models a production RAG pipeline: `add_or_update_document()` handles incremental indexing without rebuilding indexes from scratch—each match document is added with its timestamp preserved, enabling temporal queries and freshness boosting later. `retrieve_initial_candidates()` demonstrates the first retrieval stage using approximate nearest neighbor logic on embeddings and keyword filtering, achieving O(k log N) complexity rather than O(N). The `rerank_with_diversity()` method implements Maximal Marginal Relevance: as each document is selected, its similarity to already-selected documents is computed, penalizing redundant results—notice how the best result isn't selected first if a more diverse alternative exists. The `apply_freshness_boost()` method shows temporal decay: documents older than 30 days receive lower scores unless they're landmarks (World Cup final). The Reciprocal Rank Fusion formula combines embedding-based and keyword-based rankings, preventing either signal from dominating. Most importantly, `record_feedback()` and `retrieve_with_exploration()` demonstrate feedback loops and Thompson sampling: user rejections are recorded as hard negatives that permanently penalize those documents, and exploration probability allows the system to occasionally try uncertain candidates to improve future decisions. When you call `retrieve_with_exploration()`, the system chains all stages: retrieval → diversity reranking → freshness boosting → Thompson sampling exploration. This mirrors production systems like Pinecone + Qdrant where retrieval is delegated to specialized vector databases optimized for HNSW-like structures.
How It Works Under the Hood

Understanding the internal mechanics of production RAG systems reveals why naive implementations collapse under load. Vector database backends like Pinecone and Weaviate use Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) indexes that structure embeddings as a multi-layer navigable graph. During insertion, a new document embedding is inserted into the bottom layer of the graph with O(log N) complexity; the graph structure ensures that neighboring nodes in embedding space are reachable with short paths. Search starts at the top layer, greedily moving toward the query embedding, then descends to lower layers where finer-grained similarity computation occurs. This exponential time reduction is critical: exact nearest neighbor search requires comparing the query to all N embeddings (O(N) distance computations), but HNSW requires only O(log N) comparisons by pruning vast regions of the embedding space. Concurrent update performance is achieved through careful synchronization: new documents are inserted into the fast top layer first and percolated downward asynchronously, preventing the index from blocking read queries during writes. Memory efficiency is provided by Product Quantization: full 768-dimensional embeddings are compressed to 64-byte quantization codes using vector quantization, reducing memory footprint by 10-12x at the cost of 1-3% accuracy loss. The reranking stage executes on a CPU cluster where dense reranking models (like ColBERT) score all retrieved documents in parallel; GPU batching ensures that 1,000 query-document pairs are scored with a single forward pass rather than 1,000 serial passes. Feedback loop integration requires a message queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ) where user interactions are buffered and periodically aggregated into training batches for the reward model. The latency waterfall is critical: if retrieval takes 150ms and reranking takes 100ms on a 500ms generation budget, only 250ms remains for the language model to generate output—unacceptable for interactive systems. This forces RAG systems to aggressively optimize retrieval and reranking, sometimes sacrificing quality for latency. Failure modes include the "curse of the 99th percentile": 99% of queries might complete in 100ms, but the 99th percentile query (querying a rare combination of document properties) takes 5 seconds, poisoning the user experience. Production systems combat this with query cancellation policies (kill queries exceeding SLA latency) and cache warming strategies that precompute retrievals for common queries.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine a real-time cricket analytics engine that must answer live commentary questions during a Test match at 100ms latency while analyzing 1 million prior cricket videos. Exact search (comparing the live query against all 1 million videos) is impossible in 100ms—the system would lag the broadcast. Instead, the analytics engine uses a hierarchical index of cricket moments: top layer has broad categories (pace bowling, spin bowling, batting), middle layers subdivide into finer categories (fast bowling to left-handers, short-pitched deliveries), bottom layer has individual video frames. When a question arrives ("What's the last time a batter was dismissed by a slower ball in this situation?"), the engine starts at the broad layer, rapidly eliminating 800,000 irrelevant videos (those aren't slower ball dismissals). Then it drills down: among 200,000 slower ball dismissals, which involve a similar batter profile and match situation (Test cricket, right-handed opener, first 20 overs)? Finally, it scores the remaining 10-20 videos with expert analysis (dense reranking). The cost is massive: fully analyzing all 1 million videos would take 10,000ms. The hierarchical shortcut reduces it to 100ms. The system also caches answers to frequently asked questions: "How many times has Rohit Sharma fallen to short-pitched bowling this series?" is queried by multiple commentators, so it's precomputed and stored in fast memory. When Rohit plays a new innings, new videos are inserted into the system; they start at the top layer ("is this a dismissal?") and percolate downward asynchronously during commentary breaks, not blocking live queries. If a commentator asks an unusual question that requires 500ms of analysis—exceeding the 100ms SLA—the system returns partial results from the top 5 candidate videos rather than blocking for full analysis. This reveals why production RAG systems use hierarchical indexes, caching, and approximate algorithms: exact answers are computationally prohibitive at broadcast latency, requiring architectural compromises.

import numpy as np
from typing import List, Dict, Optional
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import time
from collections import defaultdict

# Advanced internal mechanics: HNSW simulation, product quantization, and reward modeling

class HNSWLayer:
    """Simulates one layer of HNSW multi-layer graph structure"""
    def __init__(self
sections.max_connections:
int = 5):
        self.layer_id = layer_id
        self.graph: Dict[str, List[str]] = defaultdict(list)  # node -> neighbors
        self.max_connections = max_connections
    
    def add_node_to_layer(self
sections.node_id:
str):
        """Add node to this layer's graph with initial empty neighbor list"""
        if node_id not in self.graph:
            self.graph[node_id] = []
    
    def add_connection(self
sections.node_b:
str):
        """Add bidirectional edge, maintaining max degree"""
        if len(self.graph[node_a]) < self.max_connections:
            self.graph[node_a].append(node_b)
        if len(self.graph[node_b]) < self.max_connections:
            self.graph[node_b].append(node_a)
    
    def greedy_search(self
sections.k:
int = 1) -> List[str]:
        """
        Greedy nearest neighbor search within this layer.
        Returns k closest nodes to query found via greedy traversal.
        """
        candidates = [start_node]
        visited = {start_node}
        
        while candidates:
            current = candidates[0]
            improved = False
            
            # Check all neighbors of current node
            for neighbor in self.graph.get(current, []):
                if neighbor in visited:
                    continue
                visited.add(neighbor)
                
                # Distance computation (negative dot product for similarity)
                dist_current = -np.dot(embeddings[query_id], embeddings[current])
                dist_neighbor = -np.dot(embeddings[query_id], embeddings[neighbor])
                
                if dist_neighbor < dist_current:  # Closer node found
                    candidates.insert(0, neighbor)
                    improved = True
                    break
            
            if not improved:
                candidates.pop(0)
        
        return list(visited)[:k]

class ProductQuantizer:
    """Compresses full embeddings to quantized codes for memory efficiency"""
    def __init__(self
sections.num_codes:
int = 64):
        self.embedding_dim = embedding_dim
        self.num_codes = num_codes
        self.subspace_dim = embedding_dim // num_codes
        self.codebooks = []  # Each codebook for a subspace
        self.initialize_codebooks()
    
    def initialize_codebooks(self):
        """Initialize k-means codebooks for each subspace"""
        np.random.seed(42)
        for _ in range(self.num_codes):
            # Each codebook has 256 entries (1 byte per subspace)
            codebook = np.random.randn(256, self.subspace_dim)
            self.codebooks.append(codebook / np.linalg.norm(codebook, axis=1, keepdims=True))
    
    def quantize(self
sections.embedding:
np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Compress embedding to quantized code (64 bytes instead of 768*4 bytes)"""
        codes = np.zeros(self.num_codes, dtype=np.uint8)
        
        for i in range(self.num_codes):
            subspace = embedding[i*self.subspace_dim:(i+1)*self.subspace_dim]
            # Find closest codebook entry
            distances = np.linalg.norm(self.codebooks[i] - subspace, axis=1)
            codes[i] = np.argmin(distances)
        
        return codes
    
    def reconstruct(self
sections.codes:
np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Decompress quantized code back to approximate embedding"""
        reconstructed = []
        for i, code in enumerate(codes):
            reconstructed.append(self.codebooks[i][code])
        return np.concatenate(reconstructed)

class RewardModel:
    """Learns from feedback to predict relevance and improve retrieval ranking"""
    def __init__(self):
        self.feedback_buffer: List[Dict] = []
        self.positive_examples = 0
        self.negative_examples = 0
        self.learned_weights: Dict[str, float] = {}  # Document-query pair -> relevance score
    
    def add_feedback(self

sections.label: bool
sections.dwell_time:
float):
        """
        Add training signal from user interaction.
        label: True if user accepted, False if rejected.
        dwell_time: seconds user spent with result.
        """
        strength = min(dwell_time / 30.0, 1.0)  # Normalize dwell time to [0, 1]
        
        self.feedback_buffer.append({
            'query': query_id

sections.label: label
sections.batch_size:
int = 32):
        """Periodically train on buffered feedback to update relevance predictions"""
        if len(self.feedback_buffer) < batch_size:
            return
        
        # Process oldest batch_size examples
        batch = self.feedback_buffer[:batch_size]
        self.feedback_buffer = self.feedback_buffer[batch_size:]
        
        for example in batch:
            pair_key = f"{example['query']}_{example['doc']}"
            # Exponential moving average of relevance scores
            current_weight = self.learned_weights.get(pair_key, 0.5)
            updated_weight = 0.9 * current_weight + 0.1 * example['weight']
            self.learned_weights[pair_key] = max(0.0, min(1.0, updated_weight))  # Clamp [0, 1]
        
        print(f"[REWARD MODEL] Trained on {batch_size} examples. "
              f"Positive: {self.positive_examples}

Negative.query_id:
str, 
                                       query_embedding: np.ndarray,
                                       top_k: int = 5) -> List[tuple]:
        """
        Retrieve using HNSW, then rerank using learned reward model.
        Demonstrates how feedback loops improve retrieval quality.
        """
        # Stage 1: HNSW hierarchical search
        candidate_ids = self.hierarchical_search(query_embedding, top_k * 2)
        
        # Stage 2: Score with reward model (learned from feedback)
        scored_candidates = []
        for doc_id in candidate_ids:
            relevance_score = self.reward_model.predict_relevance(query_id, doc_id)
            scored_candidates.append((doc_id, relevance_score))
        
        # Stage 3: Sort by learned relevance
        scored_candidates.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        
        return scored_candidates[:top_k]


def demonstrate_internal_mechanics():
    """Show how internal mechanisms work under the hood"""
    print("[INTERNAL MECHANICS DEMO]\n")
    
    # Initialize system
    retriever = LatencyOptimizedRetriever(embedding_dim=768, num_layers=4)
    
    # Insert sample cricket match documents
    documents = {
        "match_001": {"title": "Bumrah bowling analysis", "match_type": "Test"},
        "match_002": {"title": "Kohli batting masterclass", "match_type": "ODI"},
        "match_003": {"title": "Powerplay tactics T20", "match_type": "T20"},
        "match_004": {"title": "Death bowling review", "match_type": "T20"},
    }
    
    print("[HNSW INSERTION] Inserting documents into hierarchical structure...")
    for doc_id, metadata in documents.items():
        embedding = np.random.randn(768)
        retriever.insert_document(doc_id, embedding, metadata)
    
    # Demonstrate product quantization compression
    print("\n[PRODUCT QUANTIZATION] Compression demonstration...")
    sample_embedding = np.random.randn(768)
    quantized = retriever.quantizer.quantize(sample_embedding)
    reconstructed = retriever.quantizer.reconstruct(quantized)
    compression_ratio = (768 * 4) / 64  # 4 bytes per float32, 1 byte per quantized code
    reconstruction_error = np.linalg.norm(sample_embedding - reconstructed) / np.linalg.norm(sample_embedding)
    print(f"  Original embedding size: 768 floats × 4 bytes = 3072 bytes")
    print(f"  Quantized size: 64 bytes")
    print(f"  Compression ratio: {compression_ratio:.1f}x")
    print(f"  Reconstruction error: {reconstruction_error:.4f} (acceptable < 0.05)")
    
    # Demonstrate hierarchical search
    print("\n[HIERARCHICAL SEARCH] HNSW multi-layer traversal...")
    query_embedding = np.random.randn(768)
    start_time = time.time()
    results = retriever.hierarchical_search(query_embedding, top_k=2)
    search_time_ms = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
    print(f"  Found {len(results)} results in {search_time_ms:.2f}ms")
    print(f"  Top results: {results}")
    
    # Demonstrate feedback loop and reward model training
    print("\n[FEEDBACK LOOP] Recording user interactions and training reward model...")
    # Simulate user feedback
    retriever.reward_model.add_feedback("query_001", "match_001", label=True, dwell_time=15.0)
    retriever.reward_model.add_feedback("query_001", "match_002", label=False, dwell_time=2.0)
    retriever.reward_model.add_feedback("query_001", "match_003", label=True, dwell_time=10.0)
    
    print("  Feedback recorded: 2 positive (relevant), 1 negative (not relevant)")
    
    # Train reward model
    retriever.reward_model.train_batch(batch_size=3)
    
    # Demonstrate improved retrieval after learning
    print("\n[RETRIEVAL WITH LEARNED RANKING] After training on feedback...")
    reranked = retriever.retrieve_with_reward_reranking("query_001", query_embedding, top_k=3)
    print(f"  Top-3 results with learned relevance scores:")
    for rank, (doc_id, relevance) in enumerate(reranked, 1):
        print(f"    {rank}. {doc_id}: relevance={relevance:.3f}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    demonstrate_internal_mechanics()

int = 4):
        self.embedding_dim = embedding_dim
        self.layers = [HNSWLayer(i) for i in range(num_layers)]
        self.quantizer = ProductQuantizer(embedding_dim)
        self.reward_model = RewardModel()
        self.embeddings: Dict[str, np.ndarray] = {}
        self.quantized_embeddings: Dict[str, np.ndarray] = {}
        self.document_metadata: Dict[str, Dict] = {}  # Metadata for each doc
    
    def insert_document(self
Negative.metadata:
Dict):
        """
        Insert document into HNSW with product quantization.
        O(log N) insertion complexity via hierarchical insertion.
        """
        self.embeddings[doc_id] = embedding
        self.quantized_embeddings[doc_id] = self.quantizer.quantize(embedding)
        self.document_metadata[doc_id] = metadata
        
        # Insert into layers from top to bottom
        for layer_idx in range(len(self.layers)):
            self.layers[layer_idx].add_node_to_layer(doc_id)
            
            # Connect to nearest neighbors in this layer (simplified neighbor selection)
            if layer_idx == 0 or layer_idx == len(self.layers) - 1:
                # Top and bottom layers: fully connected for diversity
                for other_id in list(self.embeddings.keys())[:-1]:
                    if other_id != doc_id:
                        self.layers[layer_idx].add_connection(doc_id, other_id)
    
    def hierarchical_search(self
Negative.top_k:
int = 5) -> List[str]:
        """
        Multi-layer hierarchical search: start at top layer, descend to bottom.
        Demonstrates O(log N) search complexity of HNSW.
        """
        if not self.embeddings:
            return []
        
        # Start from arbitrary top-layer node
        current_candidates = list(self.embeddings.keys())[:1]
        
        # Descend through layers
        for layer_idx in range(len(self.layers) - 1, -1, -1):
            layer = self.layers[layer_idx]
            start_node = current_candidates[0] if current_candidates else list(self.embeddings.keys())[0]
            
            # Greedy search within layer
            candidates = layer.greedy_search(
                "__query__", 
                {**self.embeddings, "__query__": query_embedding},
                start_node,
                k=max(top_k, 5)  # Maintain beam size
            )
            current_candidates = candidates
        
        return current_candidates[:top_k]

sections.content: Pro Tip: In production RAG systems, the 99th percentile latency matters more than average latency. A system averaging 50ms but occasionally hitting 2 seconds on edge-case queries will frustrate users far more than a system consistently averaging 200ms. Implement strict query timeouts (kill queries exceeding SLA latency), cache precomputation for power-law-distributed queries, and use probabilistic data structures like HyperLogLog to detect query patterns that will be slow before they cause problems. Many production RAG systems deploy query cancellation policies that return partial results rather than blocking indefinitely.
Common Patterns & Best Practices

Production RAG systems converge on several established patterns that balance quality, latency, and operational complexity. The first pattern is the multi-stage retrieval pipeline: retrieve broadly and quickly, then rerank carefully on a small candidate set. This mirrors information retrieval systems where a naive system might score all 10 million documents, but a sophisticated system scores 100 with a cheap model, then deeply reranks the top 10. The second pattern is separation of concerns: retrieval (finding potentially relevant documents) is architecturally decoupled from generation (producing answers), allowing teams to independently optimize retrieval quality without affecting generation latency. This enables A/B testing of retriever changes without rebuilding language models. The third pattern is continuous learning from feedback: unlike static search systems, RAG systems should treat user interactions (accepted answers, rejected answers, copied snippets, chat interactions) as training signals that improve future retrieval. This requires infrastructure: message queues buffering feedback, periodic batch training of reward models, and mechanisms to safely apply learned weights without introducing distribution shifts that cause retrieval collapse. The fourth pattern is hybrid search combining dense (embedding-based) and sparse (keyword-based) retrieval: dense retrieval excels at semantic relevance but struggles with exact matches and rare entities, while sparse retrieval achieves perfect precision on keywords but misses semantically related documents. Combining both via RRF or learned fusion weights gives the best of both worlds. For example, querying "COVID-19 vaccination side effects" with dense retrieval alone might miss documents using the acronym "SARS-CoV-2" instead of "COVID-19"; hybrid search catches both. The fifth pattern is freshness-aware ranking: not all retrieved documents are equally valuable across time. A document about current treatments should rank higher than a 2-year-old document about outdated treatments, yet aging algorithms must decay freshness signals gracefully to avoid entirely eliminating old documents that provide valuable historical context.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The Indian cricket team's selection committee uses multi-stage decision-making for squad selection that mirrors RAG best practices. First, the broad filter: scouts watch all state-level cricket across India (128 teams, 1,000+ players), but don't evaluate each player deeply—they quickly identify 200 players worthy of deeper analysis using basic metrics (batting average > 35, bowling economy < 7). This is the cheap retriever stage. Second, the committee watches intensive video analysis of these 200 players, assessing technique against pace bowling, spin bowling, bouncy pitches, and slow pitches—deep but expensive evaluation. Third, they apply freshness weighting: a brilliant recent performance in the domestic T20 league counts more than a stellar performance from 18 months ago, but not to the point of completely ignoring a player with one bad series and strong historical form (temporal decay). Fourth, they use hybrid evaluation: they consider both quantitative metrics (runs per innings, economy rate) and qualitative factors (ability to handle DRS situations, experience in high-pressure matches). Fifth, they continuously learn from feedback: if a selected player consistently fails in the squad but excels in domestic cricket (distribution mismatch signal), they downweight future nominations from that state's domestic competitions. Finally, they separate concerns: squad selection is independent of captain selection; you can improve the squad composition without overloading the captain with selection decisions. This multi-stage, fresh-weighted, hybrid evaluation with continuous learning mirrors sophisticated RAG systems far better than a single ranking mechanism.
